resources.string: Biometric authentication is not available. Enroll a fingerprint or screen lock in System Settings.
File encryption with the age format
PGPonyCore on GitHub, the audited crypto core
The full PGPony app on GitHub
Every feature included — no purchases, no subscriptions, ever
Unlock the full keyring + cloud sync
Require authentication to open the app
Set up screen lock or biometric in System Settings first.
Add a Comment header to encrypted and signed messages
No comment header will be written
Get notified 30, 7, 1 days before a key expires
Notification permission denied. Reminders won\'t fire until granted.
Notifications are disabled in System Settings. Open the app\'s notification settings to re-enable.
This will permanently delete ALL keys, including private keys. This cannot be undone.
You will lose access to all encrypted messages that use these keys. There is no recovery.
Could not open the rating sheet
No email app found. Write us at %1$s
Show contextual hints again on each tab
Tips will reappear as you visit each screen
No keys needed. Anyone with this passphrase can decrypt the message — share it over a separate channel. Works with gpg -c on the other end.
No keys available. Import or generate keys first.
Output as PGP text you can paste into email or chat.
Output as a binary .pgp file — saved via the share sheet.
Recipients can verify it came from %1$s
No key pair available. Generate or import a private key first.
Produces an RFC 4880 clear-signed message that anyone with your public key can verify. No encryption — anyone can read the text.
Output a standalone signature block, separate from the message.
Enter it to sign the message.
Enter it to encrypt and sign the file.
Enter it to encrypt and sign the message.
Type or paste your message at the top, pick one or more recipients below, then tap Encrypt to produce ciphertext you can copy and send anywhere.
Password-encrypted message. Enter the passphrase to decrypt.
No key pairs available. Generate or import a private key first.
Pick a file and a signing key to produce a standalone detached signature you can share alongside it.
This key is passphrase-protected. Enter the passphrase.
Pick the original file and its detached signature (.sig or .asc) to check who signed it.
Pick both the original file and its signature.
This key is protected with a passphrase.
Verify your identity to access this message
Paste an encrypted message here — it starts with -----BEGIN PGP MESSAGE-----. PGPony will detect which of your keys can decrypt it.
Pick a file to encrypt first
Pick an encrypted file to decrypt first
Paste an encrypted or signed message
This looks like a detached signature on its own. Paste the signed content alongside it to verify, or use the verify-file flow (coming in a later update).
Couldn\'t recognize this as a PGP message. Make sure you\'ve pasted the full block including the BEGIN and END marker lines.
Could not verify signature — signer key may not be in your keyring
Could not parse the discovered key: %1$s
Tap the + button in the bottom-right to generate your first key pair or import an existing one.
OpenPGP v6 (RFC 9580). Modern format with a dedicated signing subkey and hardware-key support. Pick a v4 type if your recipients use older software.
Fast, compact elliptic-curve keys (OpenPGP v4). Widely supported — a good default.
Classic RSA keys (OpenPGP v4). Maximum compatibility; larger and slower than Ed25519.
No key data — paste, choose a file, scan, or search
Couldn\'t parse key — make sure the armored block is complete
Point camera at a PGP key QR code generated from the Exchange tab on another phone.
For email addresses, PGPony first tries Web Key Directory at the recipient\'s own domain, then falls back to keys.openpgp.org. Fingerprints go straight to keys.openpgp.org.
This key is already in your keyring. Importing will add the private material and upgrade it to a key pair.
This key is already in your keyring. Importing again won\'t change anything.
This matches a hardware key on your keyring. Importing will pair this public key with the card so you can encrypt to it and verify its signatures.
This key is published on the keyserver.
This key was not found on the keyserver.
Remove the link between this key and %1$s? The key itself will not be deleted.
Both your public and private key will be permanently deleted.
The public key will be removed from your keyring.
This exports your PRIVATE key for %1$s as armored text. Anyone who gets this file can decrypt messages sent to you AND sign messages as you. Once shared, PGPony cannot take it back.
Authenticate to export your private key
Could not write public key file — share unavailable
Could not write export file — share unavailable
Could not export private key — secret material missing or unreadable
Could not write revocation file — share unavailable
No revocation certificate available for this key
This key can no longer be used to sign new messages or selected as a recipient. It can still decrypt past messages.
Could not export public key for QR encoding
This key has no email to match against
Contacts permission is needed to link a key to a contact
This key has a passphrase. Enter it to continue.
Uploaded. Verification email sent to %1$s — click the link to make your key searchable by email.
Uploaded. Verification emails sent to %1$s — click each link to make your key searchable by email.
Already published — key is searchable by email.
Uploaded, but verification couldn\'t be triggered. Key is searchable by fingerprint only — try uploading again later.
Added but not vetted — proceed with caution
Fingerprint compared via a trusted channel
Your own key, or one you fully control
Mark how confident you are that this key really belongs to its claimed owner. Trust is a hint for you — it doesn\'t change what PGPony can encrypt or verify.
Don\'t share why — the certificate just declares the key revoked
You\'ve moved to a different key; the old one is still trustworthy
The private key may have been exposed — DO NOT trust anything signed after this date
The key is no longer used but the private key isn\'t compromised
The email / name associated with the key has changed

Generates and applies an RFC 4880 revocation certificate for %1$s. The key remains in your keyring but is marked revoked — it can still decrypt past messages, but cannot be used to sign new ones or be selected as a recipient. Once applied, this cannot be undone.
Leave blank if your key has no passphrase
Answers to common questions about PGPony.
Didn\'t find what you needed? Tap Send Feedback in Settings to email the developer directly.

PGP (Pretty Good Privacy) is a long-standing standard for encrypting messages and files so only the intended recipient can read them.

It works by giving every user a pair of keys: a public key (which you share freely) and a private key (which you keep secret). Anyone with your public key can encrypt a message for you, but only your private key can decrypt it.

PGPony implements the modern OpenPGP standard (RFC 4880 + RFC 9580), so messages you encrypt here can be read by anyone using GnuPG, OpenKeychain, or any other OpenPGP tool.
Do I need a key to use this app?

Yes — you need at least one key pair before you can encrypt or decrypt anything.

Open the Keyring tab and tap the + button. You can either Generate a new key pair (recommended for new users) or Import an existing key if you already use PGP on a desktop tool like GnuPG.

When you generate a key, give it a name and email so others can identify it, and optionally protect it with a passphrase for extra security.
What\'s the difference between My Keys and Contacts?

My Keys are your own key pairs — both the public key (used to receive encrypted messages) and the private key (used to decrypt them). These represent your identities.

Contacts are other people's public keys — just the public half. You import these to send someone an encrypted message. You can never decrypt with a contact's key because you don't have their private key.

Think of it like a postal system: you keep your own mailbox key in My Keys, and you collect everyone else's mailing addresses in Contacts.
How do I share my public key with someone?

Open the Exchange tab and select the key you want to share. You have three options:

• QR code — let the other person scan it with their PGPony, or any QR scanner.

• Share as file — sends an .asc file through any sharing app like email, Signal, or Messages.

• Copy to clipboard — paste the armored key text into a message of your own.

Sharing your public key is always safe. Sharing your private key is NOT — never export or send your private key unless you're moving it to another device you control.
How do I encrypt a message?

Open the Encrypt tab. Type or paste your message into the input area, then pick one or more recipients from your Contacts (and optionally include yourself so you can read it later).

Tap Encrypt. PGPony will produce an encrypted block of text that you can copy and send through any channel — email, SMS, Slack, anywhere.

Only the recipients you selected will be able to decrypt it.
How do I decrypt a message someone sent me?

Open the Decrypt tab and paste the encrypted message. It should start with `-----BEGIN PGP MESSAGE-----` and end with `-----END PGP MESSAGE-----`.

Tap Decrypt. If the message was encrypted to one of your keys, PGPony will show you the plaintext. If your private key is passphrase-protected, you'll be asked for the passphrase first.

You can also share an .asc/.pgp/.gpg file directly to PGPony from another app — Android will offer PGPony as a share target.
What does the armored toggle do?

ASCII-armored output produces text you can paste into an email or chat message. It looks like a block of letters and numbers wrapped between `-----BEGIN PGP MESSAGE-----` and `-----END PGP MESSAGE-----` markers.

Non-armored output is binary — smaller, but you can only send it as a file attachment.

Leave it on for messages. Turn it off only if you're encrypting a large file and want to keep the size minimal.
Can I encrypt files, not just text?

Yes. PGPony accepts files shared from any other app — the Files app, your gallery, a downloads folder, anywhere.

From the source app, tap Share and choose PGPony. The file will land in the Decrypt tab (if it's an encrypted file) or the Keyring tab (if it's a key file like a .asc).

Encrypting a file the other way around — picking a file from inside PGPony — is on the roadmap but not yet shipped. For now, paste large content as text into the Encrypt tab.
Where are my private keys stored?

On your device only. PGPony stores private keys in an encrypted Room database, protected by a master key that lives in the Android Keystore.

On modern devices the Keystore is backed by hardware security (Titan M chip on Pixel, equivalent on Samsung and other vendors), which means extracting your private keys would require physical attack on the chip itself.

Your keys never leave the device unless you explicitly export them through the Exchange tab.
What happens if I lose my passphrase?

There is no recovery. PGPony does not store your passphrase and there is no "forgot password" flow — that's the whole point of strong encryption.

If you forget the passphrase to a private key, that key is permanently inaccessible, and any messages encrypted to it can never be decrypted.

Write your passphrase down somewhere safe. A password manager is ideal. A piece of paper in a locked drawer is also fine.
Does PGPony send any data to a server?

No. Core encryption and key management happen entirely on your device. PGPony has no account system, no analytics, no telemetry, and no advertising SDKs.

The app declares the INTERNET permission so it can optionally contact OpenPGP key servers if you choose to publish or look up a key — but this is opt-in and not required to use the app.

You can verify this by checking the app's network activity in Android's Settings > Privacy > Data usage.
What does Biometric Lock actually protect?

Biometric Lock prevents anyone who picks up your unlocked phone from opening PGPony without authenticating with your fingerprint or face.

It does NOT add cryptographic protection to your keys — your private keys are already encrypted at rest. Biometric Lock is a UI gate.

You can also enable Require for Decryption, which prompts for biometric auth every time you decrypt a message. Use this if you're worried about an unlocked phone in someone else's hands.
What encryption algorithms does PGPony use?

New keys default to Ed25519 for signatures and Curve25519 for encryption — the same elliptic curves used by Signal, SSH, and TLS 1.3.

Messages are symmetrically encrypted with AES-256 in AEAD OCB mode (RFC 9580), with the session key encrypted to each recipient's public key.

PGPony can also read and import legacy RSA and DSA keys for interoperability with older OpenPGP installations, but generates only modern curve-based keys for new identities.

All cryptography is performed by Bouncy Castle 1.78.1, an industry-standard library audited and used in enterprise and government systems worldwide.
How PGPony protects your messages and keys.
PGPony implements RFC 4880 (OpenPGP) and RFC 9580 (crypto-refresh). Messages are fully interoperable with GnuPG, OpenKeychain, and any other OpenPGP-compliant tool.
New keys use Ed25519 for signing and Curve25519 for encryption — the same elliptic curves used by Signal, SSH, and TLS 1.3. Symmetric encryption uses AES-256 with AEAD OCB mode (RFC 9580).
All cryptography goes through Bouncy Castle 1.78.1, the industry-standard Java crypto library used by enterprise and government systems worldwide. PGPony does not roll its own crypto.

Your keys never leave your phone unless you explicitly share them. PGPony has no server, no account system, and no analytics. The app does not require Internet permission for any core encryption feature.
Private keys are encrypted at rest

Private keys are stored using androidx.security.crypto with a master key generated and protected by the Android Keystore. The Keystore is backed by hardware security on modern devices (Titan M, Secure Enclave, or equivalent).
Enable Biometric Lock under Settings > Security to require fingerprint or face unlock every time PGPony opens. You can also require re-authentication for every decryption operation.
Sensitive content copied to the clipboard is automatically erased after a configurable timeout (default 60 seconds) so it doesn\'t linger where other apps could read it.
Send your keys or messages to any server
Require an account, email, or phone number
Include analytics, telemetry, or ad SDKs
Use third-party crypto libraries beyond Bouncy Castle
Store passphrases — they\'re only ever used in memory
Full privacy policy and technical details: pgpony.norsehor.se/privacy
Real PGP encryption, in your pocket.\n\nSend messages that only the intended recipient can read — no servers, no accounts, no tracking.
PGP works with two keys: a public key you share freely, and a private key you keep secret.\n\nAnyone with your public key can send you encrypted messages, but only your private key can decrypt them.
Once you have someone\'s public key saved in Contacts, head to the Encrypt tab to scramble a message just for them.\n\nThe result is safe to send over any channel — email, SMS, even a public post.

The Exchange tab turns your public key into a QR code. Show it to a friend running PGPony and they can scan it directly — no typing, no copy-pasting.\n\nYou can also share keys as files through any other app.
Your keys never leave this device unless you choose to share them. PGPony has no server, no analytics, and no ads.\n\nOptionally, lock the app behind your fingerprint or face for extra peace of mind.
Or tap Next to do this later from the Keyring tab.
Manage them from the Keyring tab anytime.
Require fingerprint or face to open PGPony
We use modern Ed25519 + Curve25519 by default. You can always generate more keys later from the Keyring tab.
Protect your private key with a passphrase. If you forget it, the key is permanently inaccessible — no recovery is possible.
This usually takes a couple of seconds.
Your new key pair is ready in the Keyring tab. Let\'s keep going.
PGPony can link encryption keys to your contacts for easier identification and auto-discovery of public keys.
No contacts with email addresses were found.

Your device contacts show up here. When you import someone\'s public key on the Keyring tab and their email matches a contact, they\'ll appear linked automatically. Tap the magnifying glass next to a contact to look up their key on a key server.
Import the PGP key from the scanned QR code?
Generate a key pair first to share your public key
Pick a key here to get a QR code or shareable file. The other person scans or imports it to send you encrypted messages.
Scanned data is not a PGP key
Create unlimited key pairs for different identities — work, personal, projects. Free includes 1 key pair.
Generate modern Ed25519 signing keys with Cv25519 encryption subkeys. Compact, fast, and recommended by security experts.
Publish your public key to keys.openpgp.org so anyone can find and encrypt messages to you.
Encrypt messages for multiple recipients at once. Free allows one recipient per message.
Encrypt and decrypt any file type — documents, photos, archives. Share encrypted files securely.
You\'ve unlocked all Pro features. Thank you for supporting PGPony!
Enter a code to unlock PGPony Pro for free.

Importing this key adds it to your keyring and re-verifies the signature. The key\'s identity is not yet vetted — only the math is being checked. Trust the sender independently before relying on the verified status.
No key was found for %1$s. The signer may not have uploaded their key to a public keyserver. Ask them to share their public key directly.
The signature will be re-verified automatically.
Content decrypted but not signed — origin is unverified
%1$s · Tap to look up on keys.openpgp.org
This code has already been redeemed
PGPony Pro unlocked! (offline mode — will sync when online)
Tap to renew \"%1$s\" before it stops working.
Reminders when one of your PGP keys is approaching expiration.
Clipboard contents are visible to all installed apps until cleared.
This key allows decrypting messages sent to you AND signing as you. Treat it like a password — store it somewhere only you can reach.
Anyone with this key can verify signatures from you and encrypt messages to you.

Your key is now marked revoked in your keyring. Share the revocation certificate with anyone who has your public key so they stop encrypting new messages to it, and upload it to a keyserver to propagate the revocation broadly.
No contacts with email addresses were found on this device.
No contact has %1$s in their email list.
Notes are stored locally on this device only and are never included in exports or shares of this key.
Recipients can verify the message came from this key. Anyone with your public key for that identity will see your name as the signer.
Point camera at a PGP public key QR code
Reading subkey information from your stored keys. This only happens once after upgrading.
Couldn\'t finish the update. The app will continue anyway — some subkey details may be missing until you import or generate a new key.
What would you like to do?
Choose how to handle the shared text:
Read this PGP message with one of your keys
This looks like a PGP key — import it from the Keyring tab
Set up your first key in the main app to use Quick Action
No recipient keys in your keyring. Add a public key in the main app first.
Your file was encrypted. Share or save the encrypted copy.
No private keys available. Generate or import a key pair in the main app first.
No content was shared — nothing to act on.
Unlock PGPony to use Quick Action.
This does not look like a PGP message — paste content that starts with -----BEGIN PGP MESSAGE-----.
Most screens update immediately. Restart PGPony if some text remains in the previous language.
Choose the language you\'d like to use.\n\nYou can change this anytime in Settings.
Hold your security key to the phone
Place your YubiKey, Token2, or other OpenPGP card against the back of the phone and hold it there.
This device does not have NFC, so hardware keys can\'t be used here.
NFC is turned off. Enable NFC in system settings, then try again.
Manage the admin PIN, unblock the user PIN, or factory-reset this card.
The admin PIN (PW3) authorizes management operations. Default is 12345678. Minimum 8 digits.
If the user PIN is blocked, set a new one using the admin PIN. Minimum 6 digits.
Admin PIN must be at least 8 digits.
User PIN must be at least 6 digits.
This permanently erases ALL keys and data on the card and restores the default PINs. It cannot be undone. Only do this on a card you intend to wipe.
Are you absolutely sure? This wipes the card.
Hold your card to the back of the phone.
Card factory reset. Default PINs restored (user 123456, admin 12345678).
Create an Ed25519 signing key and a Cv25519 encryption key directly on the card. The private keys never leave the card.

This generates the keys ON the card. They cannot be backed up or exported. If the card is lost or damaged, the keys are gone for good. This also overwrites any existing keys in the card\'s signature and encryption slots.
I understand these keys cannot be backed up.
Hold your card to the back of the phone and keep it still. Generating keys takes a few seconds.
Saving the new key to your keyring…
The key was generated but could not be saved to the keyring.
Open and decrypt an existing pass store. PGPony reads it in place and never modifies it.
Ask for biometric unlock before opening the Password Store.
Remove %1$s from PGPony? This only removes the reference. Your files are not touched.
Import an existing pass (password-store) folder to browse and decrypt its entries, including with your hardware key. PGPony reads your store in place and never modifies it.
This store is not available. The folder may have moved or its permission was revoked. Re-import it from the Password Store list.
This entry could not be read. If it lives in cloud storage it may not be downloaded yet.
No key in your keyring matches this entry. Import the key it was encrypted to, then try again.
This entry is encrypted to a hardware key. Hardware-key decryption arrives in the next update.
This entry is encrypted. Decrypt it to view the password and details.
This key is protected by a passphrase. Enter it to decrypt.
Could not decrypt. Check the passphrase and try again.
Detected one-time-code URI. Code generation is not supported.
This entry is encrypted to a hardware key. Enter the card PIN, then hold the card to the back of the phone.
Hold your card to the back of the phone.
This entry needs a hardware key, but NFC is off or unavailable on this device. Enable NFC and try again.
Change the user PIN (PW1) on your hardware key. This is the PIN you enter to sign and decrypt — not the admin PIN.
PIN must be at least 6 characters.
Hold your security key to the phone…
Clear-sign a message with the card\'s signature key. Copy the output and verify it with \"gpg --verify\". The card\'s public key must already be paired in your keyring.
Hold your security key to the phone…
Enter your card\'s user PIN, then hold the card to your phone to sign.
This card has no signature key.
Pair this card\'s public key into your keyring first, then try again.
Signing while encrypting with a hardware key isn\'t supported yet — use the Sign tab to clear-sign, or choose a software key.
Paste a PGP message encrypted to this card\'s key, enter your user PIN, and hold the card to your phone to decrypt. The card\'s public key must already be paired in your keyring.
Hold your security key to the phone…
Encrypted to your hardware key \"%1$s\". Enter your PIN and tap the card to decrypt.
This message is encrypted to your hardware key. Enter your PIN and tap the card to decrypt.
Set a new expiration for %1$s. This re-signs the key and its subkeys; the fingerprint stays the same.
Enter your PIN, then hold the security key to your phone when prompted.
Could not update expiration. Please try again.
Confirm with your fingerprint before signing with a software key. Hardware keys still use PIN + tap.
Authenticate to sign with your key
